title: Aloo Gobi

overview.description: A dry North Indian sabzi of potatoes and cauliflower cooked with onion, tomato, ginger and aromatic spices.

ingredients: 500g potatoes, peeled and cut into 3cm chunks
1 medium cauliflower, cut into medium florets
1 tbsp dried fenugreek leaves (kasuri methi), crushed
method: Heat the oil in a large, heavy-based frying pan or sauté pan over medium-high heat.
Add the cumin seeds and fry until aromatic.
Add the potatoes and cauliflower.
Stir-fry for 5–7 minutes until they begin to take on a little colour.
Remove the vegetables and set aside.
Reduce the heat to medium.
Add the onion to the same pan.
Cook until softened and lightly golden.
Add the garlic, ginger and green chilli if using.
Cook for 1–2 minutes until fragrant.
Add the chopped tomatoes, turmeric, ground coriander, ground cumin, Kashmiri chilli powder and salt.
Cook until the tomatoes have broken down and the oil begins to separate from the masala.
Return the potatoes and cauliflower to the pan.
Add the water and stir well.
Cover and cook over medium-low heat until the vegetables are tender, stirring occasionally.
Remove the lid and cook for a few more minutes to evaporate any excess moisture.
Add the garam masala and crushed kasuri methi.
Stir gently to combine.
Finish with the lemon juice and fresh coriander.
Taste and adjust the seasoning before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Aloo gobi is one of the best-known North Indian vegetable dishes, with aloo meaning potato and gobi meaning cauliflower. It is a dry sabzi rather than a curry with a large amount of gravy.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to build a masala and cook vegetables so they absorb flavour while retaining their own texture.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A simple, inexpensive and highly adaptable Indian vegetable dish that makes excellent use of potatoes and cauliflower while teaching the fundamentals of building a dry sabzi.
chapman-tips: Cut the potatoes and cauliflower into similarly sized pieces so they finish cooking together.
Don't add too much water; aloo gobi should be relatively dry.
Browning the vegetables slightly before adding them to the masala adds extra flavour.
Cook the tomato masala until the raw tomato flavour has disappeared.
Stir gently once the vegetables are tender to avoid breaking up the cauliflower.
Kasuri methi adds a distinctive savoury, slightly bitter aroma and is worth including.
For a milder family version, leave the green chilli out and allow adults to add extra chilli at the table.
Aloo gobi is particularly good with a sharp pickle or chutney alongside it.
notes.batch-cooking: Good for batch cooking. The texture softens slightly after freezing and reheating, but the flavours develop well.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently in a frying pan or microwave. Leftover aloo gobi can be used as a filling for parathas or sandwiches.
